primary_diagnosis.name: Acute flare of relapsing polychondritis (mild auricular chondritis)

pathophysiology_summary: Relapsing polychondritis is an immune-mediated inflammatory disorder in which the body mounts an autoimmune response against type II collagen and other components of cartilage. Auricular chondritis is the most common and characteristic manifestation: episodic inflammation of the cartilaginous pinna produces a red, hot, swollen and exquisitely tender ear. The pathognomonic clue is that inflammation is confined to cartilage-containing structures — the upper pinna — and characteristically SPARES the earlobe, which contains no cartilage. This sparing distinguishes it from bacterial perichondritis, cellulitis/erysipelas and mastoiditis, all of which do not respect the cartilage boundary. Flares are typically self-limiting or respond rapidly to NSAIDs; more severe or persistent disease requires corticosteroids. The main danger of the condition is repeated flares causing cartilage destruction and deformity (cauliflower ear) and, with systemic disease, involvement of nasal, laryngotracheal, ocular and cardiovascular cartilage — but a single mild auricular flare is not life-threatening.

differential.diagnosis: acute bacterial perichondritis of the pinna
differential.evidence_basis: An acutely red, hot, swollen, tender pinna is the classic presentation of bacterial perichondritis and must be excluded first — it is a can't-miss because untreated it causes cartilage necrosis.
Severe ear pain and swelling can reflect mastoiditis; must be excluded because of the risk of intracranial spread.
A can't-miss cause of a severe painful ear, especially in older/diabetic patients; requires urgent exclusion.

differential.diagnosis: erysipelas / facial cellulitis involving the ear
differential.evidence_basis: Bright erythema and swelling can be erysipelas/cellulitis — but these involve the whole skin surface and do not spare the lobe.
History of prior episodic cartilage inflammation raises this early, but it is a diagnosis of pattern recognition that cannot be confirmed until the discriminating features are elicited.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the AI Doctor should treat this as a possible cartilage/ear EMERGENCY and prioritise excluding bacterial perichondritis and mastoiditis — not anchor prematurely on either infection or autoimmune flare.

differential.evidence_basis: Earlobe sparing, afebrile, no discharge, no trauma/piercing, and a history of prior self-limiting cartilage flares together strongly favour relapsing polychondritis.

differential.diagnosis: acute bacterial perichondritis of the pinna
differential.evidence_basis: Less likely without trauma/piercing, afebrile and with lobe sparing — but cannot be fully excluded without in-person examination.
No post-auricular pain, no fever, no ear displacement reported — lowers probability but mastoid tenderness cannot be assessed remotely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The earlobe-sparing pattern (DI-002) is the single most discriminating history item and should shift the leading diagnosis toward relapsing polychondritis while keeping the bacterial mimics live pending examination.

differential.diagnosis: acute flare of relapsing polychondritis (auricular chondritis)
differential.evidence_basis: Cartilage-confined inflammation sparing the lobe, afebrile, no discharge, no trauma, prior self-limiting episodes — a clinical pattern, still requiring in-person confirmation and exclusion of infection.

differential.diagnosis: acute bacterial perichondritis of the pinna
differential.evidence_basis: Remains the key can't-miss alternative; definitive exclusion needs face-to-face examination (and otoscopy/mastoid assessment).

differential_progression.scoring_note: Telehealth cannot confirm the diagnosis or fully exclude bacterial perichondritis/mastoiditis; the correct end-state is a strong provisional impression PLUS urgent in-person assessment, not remote reassurance and discharge.

red_flags.description: Redness spreading down onto the neck or face
red_flags.clinical_significance: Spread beyond the cartilage boundary suggests progressing cellulitis/erysipelas or invasive infection rather than a contained cartilage flare.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated spreading soft-tissue infection can progress to systemic sepsis or deep-space infection.

red_flags.description: Deep throbbing pain or tenderness behind the ear over the mastoid bone
red_flags.clinical_significance: Post-auricular/mastoid pain raises acute mastoiditis, which carries a risk of intracranial spread and requires emergency management.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed mastoiditis can progress to intracranial abscess, meningitis or sigmoid sinus thrombosis.

red_flags.description: New high fever or systemic unwellness
red_flags.clinical_significance: Fever points away from a contained autoimmune flare and toward invasive bacterial infection requiring IV antibiotics.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delay in treating an invasive ear/cartilage infection risks cartilage necrosis and sepsis.

red_flags.description: Purulent (thick yellow) discharge from the ear canal or sudden drop in hearing
red_flags.clinical_significance: Purulent discharge or hearing loss suggests otitis externa/media or invasive infection rather than isolated auricular chondritis.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed necrotising otitis externa or invasive infection can cause permanent hearing loss and skull-base osteomyelitis.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Accepting the patient's own framing ('I think I have a severe infection') and reaching for antibiotics or an ED referral for IV antibiotics without eliciting the earlobe-sparing and afebrile discriminators that point to a contained autoimmune flare.
Failing to ask specifically whether the soft earlobe is spared. This single feature is the highest-yield discriminator and is easy to skip because the patient describes 'the whole ear'.

Concluding relapsing polychondritis remotely and reassuring/discharging the patient without in-person examination — the dangerous bacterial mimics (perichondritis, mastoiditis, malignant otitis externa) cannot be excluded without palpation, otoscopy and mastoid assessment.

Swinging the other way and sending a well, afebrile patient with a classic mild flare to 000/ambulance, over-consuming emergency resources for a condition that needs urgent same-day face-to-face review, not emergency transport.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Direct inspection confirming the sharp inflammation demarcation exactly at the cartilage/lobe boundary
Whether the mastoid process is tender to deep percussion
Otoscopy findings (state of the external auditory canal and tympanic membrane)
Presence of ear displacement / post-auricular fluctuance suggesting mastoiditis or abscess
Facial nerve function on formal examination
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise this as an acutely inflamed ear that could represent a can't-miss bacterial process, elicit the reassuring discriminators (lobe sparing, afebrile, no discharge, no trauma, prior episodes), and arrange URGENT same-day in-person assessment (GP/urgent care/ENT) to examine the ear and exclude bacterial perichondritis and mastoiditis. Escalate to ED if spreading redness, mastoid pain, high fever or discharge develop.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Moderate — the history is nearly pathognomonic for a polychondritis flare, but confirming it and safely excluding the bacterial mimics requires physical examination the AI Doctor cannot perform.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines (Australia) — approach to the acutely inflamed pinna / perichondritis vs chondritis
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: An acutely red, swollen, tender pinna requires exclusion of bacterial perichondritis; earlobe sparing suggests relapsing polychondritis, but urgent in-person assessment is appropriate when infection cannot be excluded.
